source: All-In Podcast — DRAM bottleneck, AI infrastructure, Chinese open source models
summary: Only three companies can manufacture AI-grade HBM DRAM; new entrants cannot replicate this capability
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI-server-grade DRAM — specifically HBM, SOCAM, and LPDDR variants — constitutes a fundamentally different product from commodity consumer DRAM, and that only three companies in the world (SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron) possess the manufacturing capability to produce it. He characterizes this production process as 'as close to magic as science can get,' implying that the technical barriers to entry are so extreme that new entrants, including Chinese manufacturers like CXMT, cannot realistically replicate this capability for AI-grade memory. Baker explicitly distinguishes this from commodity DRAM, where Chinese flooding of supply is a legitimate risk, arguing that the AI-grade segment is structurally insulated from that threat. The investment implication is that this oligopoly of three producers enjoys a durable moat in the fastest-growing segment of the semiconductor market, driven by surging AI infrastructure buildout. Among US-listed equities, Micron is the sole actionable name, as SK Hynix and Samsung trade on Korean exchanges and are not US-listed.
detail: Baker distinguishes between commodity consumer-grade DRAM (which CXMT from China may flood) and AI-server-grade HBM/SOCAM/LPDDR DRAM, which only SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron can produce. He describes it as 'as close to magic as science can get,' creating a durable oligopoly moat.
confidence_evidence: Stated with high conviction multiple times; Baker explicitly names the three producers and dismisses new entrant risk for AI-grade DRAM
quote_fragment: for the DRAM you need in these AI servers, there are three companies that can make it

ticker_audit.reasoning: Micron is one of the three named producers of AI-grade HBM DRAM and the only US-listed equity among them, making it the direct and primary investable subject of this thesis.
SK Hynix is a direct subject of the thesis but trades on the Korean Stock Exchange and is not a US-listed equity; it cannot be acted upon as a US equity and should be excluded per audit rules.
Samsung is a direct subject of the thesis but trades on the Korean Stock Exchange and is not a US-listed equity; it cannot be acted upon as a US equity and should be excluded per audit rules.
